steps.prompt: Which word is the main clause verb?

hints.vobis: 'vobis' is a pronoun in the ablative/dative — here ablative after 'pro' (for you)
hints.vos: 'vos' is a pronoun meaning 'you' (nominative/accusative) — here nominative, subject of 'dormietis'
hints.dormietis: 'dormietis' is a verb but in the conditional 'si' clause

hints.to be strong: 'to be strong' is 'valere' — 'vigilare' means 'to keep watch'
hints.to conquer: 'to conquer' is 'vincere' — 'vigilare' means 'to stay awake/keep vigil'

hints.Present indicative: Present would be 'vigilo' — 'Vigilabo' with -bo ending is future
hints.Present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'vigilem' — 'Vigilabo' is future indicative
hints.Future perfect indicative: Future perfect would be 'vigilavero' — 'Vigilabo' is simple future

hints.valeo, valere, valui, valitum: These are the parts of 'valere' (to be strong/well)
hints.vinco, vincere, vici, victum: These are the parts of 'vincere' (to conquer)
hints.vivo, vivere, vixi, victum: These are the parts of 'vivere' (to live)

hints.Present indicative: Present would be 'dormitis' — 'dormietis' with -ietis ending is future
hints.Present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'dormiatis' — 'dormietis' is future indicative
hints.Imperfect subjunctive: Imperfect subjunctive would be 'dormiretis' — 'dormietis' is future indicative

hints.doleo, dolere, dolui, dolitum: These are the parts of 'dolere' (to grieve/hurt)
hints.doceo, docere, docui, doctum: These are the parts of 'docere' (to teach)
hints.dono, donare, donavi, donatum: These are the parts of 'donare' (to give/donate)

hints.Nominative: Nominative would be 'vos' — 'vobis' after 'pro' is ablative
hints.Accusative: Accusative would be 'vos' — 'pro' governs the ablative
hints.Dative: Dative is also 'vobis', but after 'pro' it is ablative meaning 'on behalf of you'

steps.prompt: What is the rhetorical contrast between 'vigilabo' and 'dormietis'?
steps.target: Cicero's tireless devotion vs. the citizens' ability to rest in safety
choices: Cicero's tireless devotion vs. the citizens' ability to rest in safety
A threat that Cicero will attack at night
Cicero admits he suffers from insomnia

hints.Cicero criticizes lazy citizens: Cicero is not criticizing — he is reassuring them that they can sleep while he watches
hints.A threat that Cicero will attack at nigh: Cicero is promising protection, not threatening attack
hints.Cicero admits he suffers from insomnia: The contrast is rhetorical — Cicero portrays himself as a devoted guardian

translation: I will keep watch on your behalf, even if you sleep.
